On May 9th, 1914, President Woodrow Wilson issued a presidential proclamation that officially established the first Mother's Day holiday to celebrate America's mothers. It's my understanding that well over 150 countries around the globe celebrate Mother's Day. It's not always on this particular day. They have various times in their particular countries on their calendars in which they celebrate Mother's Day. But we find that the idea for Mother's Day here in America is credited by some to Julia Howe and by others to Anna Jarvis. Anna Jarvis is kind of the one that's most notably known for starting Mother's Day or the idea of it. And they both suggested that there would be a holiday dedicated to a day of peace. Many individuals then started celebrating Mother's Day in 1911, but it wasn't until 1914 when Woodrow Wilson lobbied Congress that Mother's Day was officially set on the second Sunday of every May. And in his first proclamation, here's what President Woodrow Wilson said. He said that the holiday offered a chance to, quote unquote, publicly express our love and reverence for the mothers of our country. On May 9th, 1914, President Woodrow Wilson
issued a presidential proclamation
that officially established the first Mother's Day holiday
to celebrate America's mothers.
It's my understanding that well over 150 countries
around the globe celebrate Mother's Day.
It's not always on this particular day.
They have various times in their particular countries
on their calendars in which they celebrate Mother's Day.
But we find that the idea for Mother's Day
here in America is credited by some to Julia Howe
and by others to Anna Jarvis.
Anna Jarvis is kind of the one that's most notably known
for starting Mother's Day or the idea of it.
And they both suggested that there would be a holiday
dedicated to a day of peace.
Many individuals then started celebrating
Mother's Day in 1911, but it wasn't until 1914
when Woodrow Wilson lobbied Congress
that Mother's Day was officially set
on the second Sunday of every May.

here's what President Woodrow Wilson said.

quote unquote, publicly express our love and reverence
for the mothers of our country.
